title: Webinar: The Ceasefire And Mad Markets
gist: Gave and Kaletsky argue the US-Iran ceasefire reverts markets to the pre-war (Feb 2026) regime, reviving the contrarian rotation out of US tech into value, Europe, Asia and a weaker dollar, while a global capex boom keeps the world in an inflationary boom with rising real rates and resilient credit. They flag China-controlled oil ($60-100 band), record-cheap Chinese internet and Japanese-yen valuations as the biggest mispricings to exploit.

insights.claim: The US-Iran ceasefire (signed in Versailles) is a durable end to the war because Trump is the party that backed down and wants it over, removing the risk of Iran restarting hostilities.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: If the war is genuinely over, the three-month countertrend correction reverses; position for a resumption of pre-war trends.
insights.quote: the reason I think it really is over is that Trump clearly wants it over. He's the one who's backed down.
insights.locator: Now, they're likely to resume from an investment standpoint

insights.claim: There are no actual reparations flowing from the US government to Iran; instead Iran will receive investment mostly from neighboring Gulf states plus some US private capital, while the US essentially restores the pre-2016 nuclear-deal status quo (a USMCA-style rebranding of the old GCPOA/JCPOA).
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Reframes headline 'reparations' as Gulf-funded reconstruction, supporting a constructive read on Middle East stability rather than a US capitulation.
insights.quote: There are no reparations going to Iran from the U.S. government.
insights.locator: And this is where actually I would slightly differ

insights.claim: Israel is a junior partner that would like to sabotage the deal but has no agency to do so for the next few months given the US wants the war ended.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Discount headlines suggesting Israeli action could reignite the conflict and derail the rotation trade.
insights.quote: the U.S. is the senior partner and Israel is very much a junior partner. They would like to sabotage this deal if they possibly can. But I can't see any way that they'll be able to
insights.locator: There is a third one, Israel

insights.claim: Most of the investment community does not believe the war is over, and this skepticism is itself a hallmark of genuine game-changing transformations (as with Bear Stearns/Lehman in 2008 and TARP/QE bottoming the market), meaning the reversion trade is not yet priced in.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: The mispricing window persists for weeks; act before consensus accepts the ceasefire.
insights.quote: the skepticism in markets and in the media is invariably an aspect of that
insights.locator: And I think the reason it certainly hasn't been in the last few days

themes.theme: Pre-war regime resumes: rotation out of US tech into value

insights.claim: The world is reverting financially to where it stood on Feb 27, 2026, reviving the three pre-war trends: rotation out of US into Europe/Asia, rotation out of growth into value, and higher rates/inflation.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Core allocation call: underweight US/tech, overweight ex-US value, stay short duration.
insights.quote: the world is now basically going back pretty much from a financial standpoint, pretty much to where it was on February 27th before the war broke out
insights.locator: And the things that really performed in the six, nine months

insights.claim: In the 15 months from Trump's Nov 2024 election to end-Feb 2026, US equities were the weakest major market in the world and the dollar the weakest currency, with US stocks underperforming Europe and Japan by about 20 percentage points (the biggest US relative underperformance since 2000).
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: The 'US exceptionalism' consensus was a reassessment of overweight (70% was too high, 50% still overweight), not a flight; expect renewed de-rating of US weighting.
insights.quote: It underperformed both Europe and Japan by about 20 percentage points.
insights.locator: The U.S. stock market went up, but it wasn't the strongest

insights.claim: The reversion trade is now once again contrarian and available at a much more attractive level than in Nov 2024, because the three-month war correction was a massive countertrend rally in US-vs-world and tech-vs-everything.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Treat the post-war setup as a fresh entry point for the ex-US value rotation, not a chase.
insights.quote: this is a really big investment opportunity. And it's now, again, a contrarian one
insights.locator: Now, they're likely to resume from an investment standpoint from a much more attractive level

insights.claim: During the war, capital herded into an ever-narrowing slice of the tech sector (the Mag 7 themselves only went sideways, down ~8% since their October high) as investors sought businesses they believed immune to macro shocks.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Crowding into sub-segments of tech is a contrarian sell signal as macro fears fade.
insights.quote: people have been looking for smaller and smaller sub-segments of the tech sector, which have not yet sort of exploded
insights.locator: Now, rightly or wrongly, I think wrongly

insights.claim: The three-month surge in semiconductor stocks is a sign of market madness rather than fundamentals.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Fade extreme semiconductor strength; it is the most cyclical part of tech being mispriced as non-cyclical.
insights.quote: what's happened to semiconductor stocks in the last three months is insane. That's a, it's a sign of madness
insights.locator: And I think this is why we were going to talk about this being a period of market madness

insights.claim: China, as the largest oil consumer and importer with huge inventory capacity, now sets the oil price by buying aggressively near $60-65 and stopping near $100, pinning crude in a roughly $60-100 band.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Trade oil as range-bound ($60-100), not as a runaway commodity; the biggest buyer caps and floors it.
insights.quote: each time oil gets close to 60 bucks, 65, 60, China ships in as much as it can
insights.locator: And it controls the oil price because it has this huge

insights.claim: A stable $60-100 oil band is strongly bullish for energy equities because oil companies make strong profits, stay capital-disciplined, buy back shares and pay big dividends in that range rather than over-drilling at high prices.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Own energy equities for the disciplined-capital-return story, not on hopes of $150 oil.
insights.quote: between 60 and 100, they're actually decently disciplined with capital
insights.locator: And even better is between 60 and 100

insights.claim: Over the past five years energy has been the second-best performing S&P 500 sector (almost matching tech), and it delivers outperformance precisely when other sectors do not (notably 2022 and 2026).
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Energy is a high-return diversifier with negative correlation to the rest of the market; hold it as ballast.
insights.quote: over the past five years, energy stocks have done almost as well as tech stocks. Their second best performing sector on the S&P 500
insights.locator: Between 60 and 100 bucks, they actually buy back shares

insights.claim: Because all inventories were drawn to near-zero during the crisis, oil will not collapse from here; a global inventory-rebuild phase supports prices despite the ceasefire.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Don't bet on a post-ceasefire oil crash; near-term restocking demand underpins prices.
insights.quote: everybody drew down their inventories to zero. So we're now going to be in a phase where everybody's going to have to rebuild inventories
insights.locator: Having said that, I do tend to believe that

insights.claim: China will not let oil crash to $30-50 partly because it does not want to bankrupt its geopolitical ally Russia, reinforcing a managed floor.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Geopolitics adds a hard floor under oil; the Russia-China alliance is a structural support for crude.
insights.quote: Russia is an important ally, so they don't want to bankrupt Putin either
insights.locator: I also agree with you that they will not think it's in their interest

themes.theme: Energy capex: alternatives, nuclear and oil services

insights.claim: A stable, sufficiently high oil band is a very good environment for alternative energy (nuclear, coal, wind, solar) because their marginal generating cost beats fossil fuels while energy stability limits recession and windfall-tax risk.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Alternatives have more upside than fossil-fuel producers from here, having collapsed until a year ago; buy the laggard recovery.
insights.quote: I think they actually have more upside than the fossil fuel companies themselves
insights.locator: They've done very well in the last 12 months

insights.claim: Oil-service and energy-service businesses are underestimated beneficiaries of a massive coming reallocation of energy infrastructure (pipelines, Iran reconstruction, Gulf ports), potentially safer than straight fossil-fuel producers.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Tilt energy exposure toward services/infrastructure to capture the rebuild capex with lower commodity-price risk.
insights.quote: I think there's a lot of interesting opportunities there, which in some ways I think may be safer and more reliable than the straight fossil fuel producers
insights.locator: The other area which I think is underestimated

insights.claim: Iran will be rebuilt, with roughly EUR 300 billion of reconstruction spending coming largely from the Gulf, and pipelines being built that will eventually make the Strait of Hormuz largely irrelevant (over ~5 years).
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: A multi-hundred-billion reconstruction wave is a multi-year theme for engineering, materials and services exposed to the Gulf.
insights.quote: Iran's going to be rebuilt. That's where the euro 300 billion is really going to be spent
insights.locator: Iran, if I'm right, and this piece really does hold

insights.claim: Countries will adopt China's 'more of everything' energy strategy for national-security (not green) reasons; India in particular will regret blocking cheap Chinese solar panels and EVs with high tariffs while holding $700bn of US Treasuries.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Expect a global, security-driven build-out of diversified energy; watch policy shifts in India and other importers.
insights.quote: It's just more of everything for national security imperatives
insights.locator: So I do think coming out of this

insights.claim: China is the short list (effectively the top name) for who can actually build Gulf pipelines and ports today, given engineering, steel and tungsten capacity; the new UAE-to-Amman pipeline is being built by the Chinese.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Chinese engineering/EPC and materials firms are prime beneficiaries of Gulf infrastructure capex.
insights.quote: It's being built by the Chinese. And so there's a little bit of, if you're Saudi Arabia
insights.locator: Right now, you look at the new pipeline that the UAE is building

themes.theme: Inflationary boom and the capital-demand quadrant

insights.claim: The world is in a full inflationary boom, not a deflationary peace dividend, because wars are inflationary but the post-war environment is dominated by gargantuan, broad-based demand for capital.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Do not buy bonds on a 'peace = deflation' thesis; position for sustained inflation and rising real rates.
insights.quote: if truth is the first casualty of war, bonds are a close second
insights.locator: Now, usually wars are inflationary

insights.claim: Simultaneous capex booms across reconstruction, defense, electricity grids and AI data centers (McKinsey: $6.7 trillion of data-center spend by 2030) create overwhelming demand for capital that forces real rates higher via crowding out.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Multi-trillion capital demand is structurally bullish real rates and inflation; underweight long-duration bonds.
insights.quote: According to McKinsey, it's $6.7 trillion between now and 2030
insights.locator: Obviously, we've got all the data center spending needs

insights.claim: Global defense spending is ratcheting up broadly: the US moving from ~$1 trillion toward $1.5 trillion and Europe from under EUR 200 billion to EUR 380 billion.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Sustained defense outlays add to the inflationary capex wave and support defense/industrial equities.
insights.quote: The U.S. saying, oh, we're going to move from one trillion to one and a half. Europe saying we're going to move from less than 200 billion to 380 billion
insights.locator: We've got that. We've got every country announcing

insights.claim: An inflationary bust (a 1974-style bear market in everything) is now off the table because the war risk that could push oil above $100 has receded; the path is a continued inflationary boom rather than a benign disinflationary boom.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Rule out the worst stagflationary scenario; favor inflation-beneficiary assets while staying cautious on bonds.
insights.quote: I think we can completely forget about the inflationary bust scenario
insights.locator: Now, with if I'm right, if we're right, that the war has now ended

insights.claim: Surplus countries are increasingly recycling reserves into physical commodity stocks (oil, food, minerals) rather than US Treasuries, adding to inflationary dynamics; China even maintains a frozen-pork reserve ('piggy bank').
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Diversification away from Treasuries into hard assets is structurally inflationary and supportive of commodities.
insights.quote: instead of investing the reserves in US treasuries, they're going to invest that in oil stocks and food stocks and mineral stocks
insights.locator: And this is a great point you've been making

insights.claim: The US is running budget deficits of around 7% of GDP, potentially rising toward 9%, making a deflationary bust hard to envisage.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Persistent large fiscal deficits underpin nominal growth and inflation; favor real assets over nominal bonds.
insights.quote: the U.S. running budget deficits of seven percent of GDP, potentially going to nine
insights.locator: And I struggle to see a bust

insights.claim: Long copper / short Bitcoin is the best trade for the year; copper is the most attractive metal even at all-time highs as the biggest beneficiary of the capex boom in grids and data centers.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Copper is the prime metal play on electrification capex; the long copper / short Bitcoin pair expresses it.
insights.quote: My trade was long copper, short Bitcoin, which I still believe in
insights.locator: And for the record, you know, I started the year

insights.claim: Gold's bull market will restart (driven by private savings rotating out of US-dollar assets), but gold may be the least attractive precious metal because central-bank buying is likely paused and real rates are rising; silver and copper offer more upside.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Stay long gold but prefer silver and copper within the metals complex for capex leverage.
insights.quote: I think the gold bull market will restart. It just might not be the best metal to own
insights.locator: Now, to be very clear, I actually think that gold does go higher here

insights.claim: The loss of US Navy control over global sea lanes undermines part of the case for central-bank gold buying, since a key value of gold is convertibility into needed commodities (wheat, rice) that secure shipping enables.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: A novel bearish-for-central-bank-gold lens; watch whether reserve managers pivot to physical commodity stockpiles instead.
insights.quote: the fact that the US Navy no longer controls the world's sea lanes shifted that
insights.locator: Obviously, the fact that the US Navy no longer controls

themes.theme: China and Hong Kong equities: a Hong Kong problem

insights.claim: Chinese equity weakness is really a Hong Kong problem: over the past year Shenzhen outperformed the S&P 500 and Shanghai roughly matched it, while the Hong Kong-listed H-share (foreigner-accessed) index fell, so local investors have done fine.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Distinguish onshore China (working) from offshore Hong Kong (broken); the dislocation is access-driven, not fundamental.
insights.quote: Actually, Shenzhen is up more than the S&P 500, and Shanghai is up about the same as the S&P 500 over the past 12 months
insights.locator: So I think what's interesting in China right now

insights.claim: Micron's market cap now exceeds the entire Chinese internet sector (Alibaba, Tencent, Baidu, Meituan, JD, Pinduoduo combined), an extreme valuation dislocation.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: A glaring relative-value gap: a third-tier cyclical chip maker valued above all of Chinese internet flags deep Chinese-tech mispricing.
insights.quote: the market cap of Micron is bigger than the whole Chinese internet sector today
insights.locator: Yeah. So if you want to really pour salt in my wounds

insights.claim: The recent H-share selloff was triggered by China's May crackdown on capital outflows via brokers (fines on Futu and Tiger), 'killing the chickens to scare the monkeys' (AIA, Manulife, Ping An, HSBC, Standard Chartered) and reviving foreign investors' regulatory PTSD.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: The selloff is a policy/sentiment shock, not a fundamentals shock; the chicken-monkey signal warns the bigger players, not systemic risk.
insights.quote: kill the chickens to scare the monkeys
insights.locator: Essentially, you remember that in May

insights.claim: Beijing actually wants onshore A-shares to go up (as shown by its 2024 and 2025 interventions) to repair consumer confidence, which is China's biggest problem despite the lowest cost of capital, labor and electricity in the world.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Policy support is biased toward onshore equities and keeping savings at home; favor A-shares over offshore proxies.
insights.quote: China today has the lowest cost of capital in the world. It has the lowest cost of labor by far, and it has the lowest cost of electricity
insights.locator: And I think the end result is the reason China's doing this

insights.claim: Alibaba and Tencent are now deep value stocks trading around 10x EBITDA (historic lows that have always rebounded), priced as ex-growth; if they fail to rebound it implies a structural regime change, but Tencent's WeChat/WePay toll on Chinese commerce makes that hard to believe.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Chinese internet at trough valuations is a contrarian buy; watch for government action or buyback/dividend catalysts at these levels.
insights.quote: they're back to 10 times EBITDA, which historically has been their low
insights.locator: Well, so here's here's the and this is actually right now the quandary

insights.claim: The renminbi kept appreciating ~40-50 basis points a month throughout the Iran crisis (rather than being frozen as in past crises), making Chinese government bonds the only major bond market to deliver positive returns through the war.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Chinese government bonds plus a rising RMB are the new portfolio diversifier where US Treasuries failed; consider RMB-denominated duration.
insights.quote: the renminbi just goes flat as a pancake against the US dollar. Not this time.
insights.locator: The other is that in the past, each time there was uncertainty

insights.claim: Chinese macro is a continuation of recent years: strong exports, solid industrial production, rapid move up the value chain, a weak consumer, but real estate is bottoming in the key cities (Shanghai, Hangzhou, Shenzhen) even as third/fourth-tier oversupply persists.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: A real-estate bottom in tier-1 cities is an underappreciated positive; watch for it to stabilize confidence and onshore assets.
insights.quote: real estate seems to be making a bottom in the big cities. But it's not imploding.
insights.locator: The macro trends have been a continuation

themes.theme: Debt sustainability and the inflation solution

insights.claim: We are only about a tenth of the way into a multi-decade uptrend in nominal and real rates that began in 2022, reversing the 40-year downtrend that ran from 1982 to 2022, so historical experience offers little guide.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Treat rising rates as a structural multi-decade regime, not a cyclical blip; build portfolios for a secular bond bear market.
insights.quote: the downtrend in both nominal and real interest rates began in 1982. It lasted basically exactly 40 years till 22. And now we've been four years into an uptrend, but maybe we're that suggests maybe we're only a 10th of the way
insights.locator: Well, you know, I agree with you that that's one of the most puzzling

insights.claim: Higher US mortgage rates (now ~6.5%, heading toward 7%) reliably crowd out housing and construction, which is the easiest sector for rising rates to squeeze, but the open question is whether higher inflation makes a 7% rate less damaging this time.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Housing/construction is the most vulnerable sector to the rate regime; watch the 7% mortgage threshold as a stress marker.
insights.quote: each time the US mortgage rate has hit 7%, each time that's happened, the Fed has done something
insights.locator: Because I look at historically

insights.claim: The only viable resolution to high government debt is higher inflation plus stronger real growth (nominal GDP at 6-7% rather than 4%), since major economies like the US and Eurozone cannot devalue their way out as small open economies (Sweden, Canada) did.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Policy will engineer higher nominal GDP; own assets that benefit from financial repression and inflation, avoid long nominal bonds.
insights.quote: the only solution to this ultimately is going to be higher inflation
insights.locator: So that's why I believe that the only solution

insights.claim: Government borrowing will not be crowded out by stronger growth because deficits keep rising, and the US Social Security Trust Fund runs out in 2031, which will be met with still more borrowing.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Expect ever-larger Treasury issuance into the 2030s, reinforcing the case for higher rates and inflation.
insights.quote: the Social Security Trust Fund runs out in 19, in 2031
insights.locator: But the classic thing that we're also crowd out

insights.claim: Corporate credit spreads are extremely tight and may stay tight, because in an inflationary environment corporates grow sales and service debt more easily; credit risk is a better fixed-income bet than duration risk.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Within fixed income, take credit risk over duration risk; spreads can stay tight in an inflationary boom.
insights.quote: in a broadly inflationary environment, paying back your debt for a corporate is much easier than a deflationary environment
insights.locator: And so that's, you know, one encouraging factor

insights.claim: Japan is economically in its best position since the late 1980s, undergoing a positive regime change (out of deflation, sensible looser fiscal policy, a coming defense-spending boom) that is the mirror image of the negative inflation/rate regime change in the US and Europe.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Japan is a structural bull case; mild inflation is comfortable for Japan and supports equities and consumption.
insights.quote: Japan is economically, leaving aside the markets, is, I think, unquestionably in a better place now than it's been at any time since the late 1980s
insights.locator: Well, you know, I think that Japan is economically

insights.claim: The yen is the most undervalued any G7 currency has ever been since Bretton Woods broke down, roughly 20% cheap in nominal terms but 50-60% cheap in real terms once 30 years of Japanese deflation versus foreign inflation is accounted for.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Extreme yen undervaluation is a high-conviction setup; size a long-yen view against dollar and euro.
insights.quote: it is the most undervalued that any G7 currency has ever been since the Bretton Woods system broke down, by a long way
insights.locator: Because the currency, as you said, is stupidly overvalued

insights.claim: The end of the war could be the catalyst for a major trend change in the yen against the dollar and euro, since the yen had begun appreciating for ~two weeks in late January/February before the war reversed it; the persistently weak yen is a sign of market madness.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Watch for a yen trend reversal as the post-war catalyst; the trade had begun before the war interrupted it.
insights.quote: the end of the war could turn out to be the catalyst for a major trend change in the yen against the dollar and the yen against the euro
insights.locator: I think the trend was actually about to change

insights.claim: The Bank of Japan is uniquely solvent because its QE bought equities, not just zero-yield bonds, so its equity gains likely exceed its bond losses, leaving Japan without the insolvency problem facing other central banks.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Dismiss the BOJ-insolvency fear; its balance sheet is a strength, removing a tail risk for Japanese assets.
insights.quote: the Bank of Japan, unlike every other central bank, when it was doing QE, was not just buying bonds over at zero or negative interest rates, it was buying equities
insights.locator: It doesn't have, uh, an insolvency problem

themes.theme: Europe, UK and the value/cyclical case

insights.claim: Europe is well-positioned sectorally for a renewed inflationary boom because financials, industrials, consumer discretionary, commodities and energy dominate its indices and benefit from rising rates and stronger activity.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Overweight Europe for the cyclical/value rotation; its sector mix is leveraged to the inflationary boom.
insights.quote: sectorally, Europe starts to look good because financials do well from rising interest rates
insights.locator: No, absolutely. Well, that's exactly the point I was going to make

insights.claim: Europe is now a more cyclical market than Asia because semiconductors (the most cyclical stocks) are no longer treated as cyclical, so removing chips leaves Korea non-cyclical while Europe retains genuine cyclical value.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: For pure cyclical-value exposure to the boom, Europe beats Asia ex-chips.
insights.quote: Europe is intrinsically a more cyclical market and actually now a much more cyclical market even in Asia
insights.locator: because Europe is intrinsically a more cyclical market

insights.claim: Cyclical value has been repriced away in the US (banks above book, no industrial value stocks, Caterpillar up 3x the Mag 7, Ford doubled on AI-beneficiary hopes) but remains cheap in Europe, where banks trade at 50-70% of book.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Cheap cyclical value (banks, industrials, autos) is only available outside the US; buy European/Chinese, not US, value.
insights.quote: European banks are still trading between 50 and 70 percent of book value
insights.locator: So, U.S. banks are all trading at well above book value

insights.claim: European financials have started outperforming the world MSCI, potentially a new trend that could drive European and UK index outperformance given banks' heavy weight in those benchmarks.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Bank outperformance is a possible engine of European/UK relative returns in a steepening-curve, inflationary-boom world.
insights.quote: it looks like banks are getting themselves off the floor
insights.locator: One of the interesting developments is you're starting to see

insights.claim: European political upheaval (French election, imminent UK government change, weak German government) is putting off investors but is not investment-significant, unlike the geopolitical changes of recent years.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Don't let European political noise deter the value/cyclical allocation; it is not a real investment risk.
insights.quote: the kind of political uncertainty we're going to see in Europe over the next year is not actually investment significant
insights.locator: The negative is that Europe is going into a period of political upheaval

themes.theme: Policy priorities: US stocks vs China stability

insights.claim: Trump's revealed priority is high US stock prices ('oil down, stocks up' on signing the MOU), and he will accept currency debasement to get there, whereas China prioritizes a stable currency and low cost of capital over its stock market.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: US policy supports equities at the cost of the dollar; China supports the currency at the cost of stocks; trade the implied dollar weakness and RMB strength.
insights.quote: as he puts down his spend, he signals and he says, oil down, stocks up. That's the first thing he said.
insights.locator: You know, one of the fascinating things, if you watch the video

insights.claim: China's preference for a stable, steady currency over higher stock prices may prove to be an advantage rather than a policy mistake as the world moves into an inflationary regime where currency stability is eventually prized.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: A stable RMB could become a sought-after store of value in an inflationary world; watch for a reassessment of Chinese currency policy.
insights.quote: if we are moving into an inflationary world, having a stable, steady currency is might actually investors might start, they haven't cared for that lately. But, but, but there is a day when
insights.locator: And I think in China, it's always been the reverse
